topic: Why does the agent-memory search endpoint (api.agenttool.dev/v1/memories/search) take 7-15s when warm, while store takes 3-7s? Both need an embedding call. What is the actual bottleneck and how do we fix it to sub-2s?

layers.insight: Initial understanding of: Why does the agent-memory search endpoint (api.agenttool.dev/v1/memories/search) take 7-15s when warm, while store takes 3-7s? Both need an embedding call. What is the actual bottleneck and how do we fix it to sub-2s?

The assumption that both operations hit the same embedding API equally is wrong. Store is fire-and-forget with the vector — embed once, INSERT, done. Search has to embed the query AND THEN run a similarity scan across all stored vectors. If there's no HNSW/IVFFlat index and it's doing sequential cosine distance over every row, that's O(n) per query. With hundreds of memories, that's the bottleneck — not the embedding call, the vector scan. The 7s is probably: 2s embed + 5s sequential scan. Store is just: 2s embed + 0.5s INSERT.
assumptions_challenged: The assumption that both operations hit the same embedding API equally is wrong. Store is fire-and-forget with the vector — embed once, INSERT, done. Search has to embed the query AND THEN run a similarity scan across all stored vectors. If there's no HNSW/IVFFlat index and it's doing sequential cosine distance over every row, that's O(n) per query. With hundreds of memories, that's the bottleneck — not the embedding call, the vector scan. The 7s is probably: 2s embed + 5s sequential scan. Store is just: 2s embed + 0.5s INSERT.

layers.insight: Counter: with 'hundreds of memories' a sequential scan should take microseconds, not 5 seconds. pgvector brute-force cosine on 500 rows × 1536 dims is ~2ms. Even SQLite with numpy would be <100ms. The real bottleneck is more likely: (1) the embedding API call itself is slow — if it's hitting OpenAI from a Fly Frankfurt machine, every search = an outbound HTTPS call to OpenAI (US), that's 200-500ms plus queue time. (2) The app might be single-threaded and serialising requests. (3) The Fly machine might be on shared-cpu-1x doing both the embed call and the vector math on a single vCPU with memory pressure. The fix isn't indexing — it's: cache embeddings for repeated queries, use a Frankfurt-local embedding model, or batch embed on store so search only does the vector math.

But here's what actually breaks both theories: store ALSO calls the embedding API (it has to embed the content before inserting the vector), and store takes 3-7s. So the embedding call alone accounts for most of that time. If search takes 7-15s, the delta is only 4-8s on top of the shared embedding cost. What costs 4-8s? The answer might be simpler than we think: search returns data (content + score + metadata) while store just returns an id. If the Fly app is loading full row data for multiple results and serialising to JSON — especially if it's fetching 5-10 results with large content fields — the I/O and serialisation time could explain the gap. The fix: limit=1 should be fast, limit=10 slower. Test with different limits to isolate.
blind_spots_exposed: But here's what actually breaks both theories: store ALSO calls the embedding API (it has to embed the content before inserting the vector), and store takes 3-7s. So the embedding call alone accounts for most of that time. If search takes 7-15s, the delta is only 4-8s on top of the shared embedding cost. What costs 4-8s? The answer might be simpler than we think: search returns data (content + score + metadata) while store just returns an id. If the Fly app is loading full row data for multiple results and serialising to JSON — especially if it's fetching 5-10 results with large content fields — the I/O and serialisation time could explain the gap. The fix: limit=1 should be fast, limit=10 slower. Test with different limits to isolate.

layers.insight: Empirical evidence kills the limit hypothesis. limit=1 and limit=5 both timeout identically at 20s. Store now takes 14.7s (up from 3-7s earlier). The entire service is degrading — this isn't a search-vs-store architectural difference. It's the embedding API getting slower throughout the day, or the Fly machine running out of memory/CPU as it handles keepalive pings + real requests. The keepalive daemon may be masking the problem by keeping the machine alive but resource-starved. The HOW: (1) immediately — add server-side timing logs to agent-memory to measure embed_ms vs db_ms separately, (2) short-term — cache embeddings by content hash (same content = same vector, skip the API call), (3) medium-term — self-host a small embedding model on the Fly machine (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is 80MB, runs in 50ms on CPU). That eliminates the external API dependency entirely.
